answers.intake_q1: When did your back pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly your back pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or trauma to your back or ribs?
answers.intake_q4: Any fever, chills, or unexplained weight loss since the pain started?
answers.intake_q5: Any shortness of breath or chest pain associated with the back pain?
answers.intake_q6: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your legs or feet?
answers.intake_q7: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your back pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a7: I tried tylenol but it didn't help very much
answers.intake_q8: Any history of kidney problems, urinary symptoms, or recent infections?
answers.intake_q9: Any pain with deep breaths or coughing?
answers.intake_q10: Any history of osteoporosis, cancer, or recent unexplained fractures?

note.soap:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with left side lower rib cage back pain that started 3 weeks ago. She denies any recent injury or trauma, fever, chills, unexplained weight loss, shortness of breath, chest pain, numbness, tingling, weakness in the legs or feet, pain with deep breaths or coughing, history of kidney problems, urinary symptoms, recent infections, osteoporosis, cancer, or unexplained fractures. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) without significant relief.
O: Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vitals available.
A: The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal back pain localized to the left lower rib cage, likely mechanical in nature given the absence of red flag symptoms or systemic signs.
Differential:
1. Musculoskeletal strain or sprain
2. Intercostal muscle strain
3. Costochondritis
4. Early presentation of rib fracture (less likely without trauma). Patient is not pregnant.

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs contraindicated or not tolerated
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid NSAIDs if history of renal or liver disease (none reported), monitor for gastrointestinal side effects
- Return precautions: Return if fever, worsening pain, neurological symptoms, chest pain, shortness of breath, or new urinary symptoms develop
- Testing: No imaging or labs indicated at this time given absence of red flags and symptom duration less than 6 weeks

evidence.title: NIAMS: Living With Back Pain (patient-friendly NIH resource)
evidence.snippet: Acute back pain happens suddenly and usually lasts a few days to a few weeks. Back pain that lasts longer than 12 weeks is called chronic back pain.   Who Gets Back Pain? Image Anyone can have back pain. You may be more likely to have back pain because of the following: Age: Back pain is more common as you age. Fitness level: Back pain is more common if…

evidence.title: Back Injuries | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: extending from your neck to your pelvis. Back injuries can result from sports injuries, work around the house or in the garden, or a sudden jolt such as a car accident. evidence.title: AAFP Choosing Wisely: Imaging for Low Back Pain (Red Flags)
evidence.snippet: Information Low back pain is one of the most common reasons for an outpatient visit. The evaluation for low back pain should include a complete, focused medical history looking for red flags, which include, but are not limited to: severe or progressive neurologic deficits (e.g., bowel or bladder function), fever, sudden back pain with spinal tenderness, trau…
